item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm coming to this article 9 months after its picture was removed, and i can't help but feel like it's lost so much without the image. the part at the end literally doesn't make sense. if we can't find a new image, this may be a rewrite candidate.

item.title: Drats - no more picture
item.content:encoded: I missed seeing the photo by one day. Hopefully someone can find a compliant replacement? As it is, the note at the end (do not edit file) conflicts with the (see image) part of the file.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apologies for the necromancy, but duty calls.

That seems a bit too vague. Would people stop believing in platypus and in weird new fashion trends?

This actually happens all the time without help. Check out Aluminum Christmas Trees on tvTropes for a bunch of great examples. I personally had always assumed that "wildberry" flavored stuff was code for "berry-like artificial flavoring #24e". Turns out it's a marketing name for a sweeter relative of the cranberry, the Lingonberry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Actually, i like it after reading it a couple of times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already don't believe in weird new fashion trends. I just see people dressed like idiots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm neutral on this one for now.

that causes complete rejection of evidence supporting occurrences and beings outside the affected human's sense of "normal."

That seems a bit too vague. Would people stop believing in platypus and in weird new fashion trends?

Identified vectors for SCP-2000 activation include visuals…

From the description I was confused as to whether the original effect is triggered by the aliens or if that is just an example. The way I understand from reading the whole story, there is no definitive proof that this effect originates from the aliens, just a strong presumption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, this wouldn't be classed as its own skip. It is rather obviously a newly discovered feature of grays, already on file and designated SCP-Blackbox. This article would be an addendum to that file.

The idea also strikes me as similar to The Silence of Dr. Who. Not enough to think they are a derivative, but the impression was there and colored my reading.

So, while I generally like the concept and find no flaw in the writing, I'm down voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fake and gay
RONPAUL2012!

aaaaaaa. Get it? Get it? Cuz the SCP makes people think it's fake. Apparently at some point I upvoted this, so I must have found it good, but I say now that I think it needs more stuff written into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I don't really care for this. It just doesn't seem very well thought out, and I'm not sure why exactly alien autopsy videos make any sense as a vector.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand the objection. It isn't, however, an immediate permanent effect. Unless you're a fan of one of a handful of specific media pieces, you can probably count on one hand the number of times you've seen something that looked like a Gray. And it's only really prevalent in high-technology countries. The vast majority of the world isn't currently at risk of being exposed to this, and so disinformation campaigns are still extremely pertinent.

I'm sorry that the article wasn't all that enjoyable for you. Go read Deus Ex Machina, it'll take the taste outta your… uh… brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that you were going for making the reader be "infected" by the meme, but I had to downvote. Now, logic is not my strong suit and it's possible I haven't considered all the ramifications of this article, but as I was reading it, all I could think was, "If disbelieving anomalies is itself an anomaly, then what's the point of the Foundation?" I suppose "What's the point of disinformation campaigns?" is closer to my issue with the concept.

Because if this exists, then all we need the Foundation for is containing the really dangerous stuff. All the half-cats and men made out of colors and monsters that just watch you from behind a window can stay in place in the world at large because they don't hurt anyone and if anyone encounters them, they'll just naturally convince themselves they're not real. (And if they don't, hey, recruit 'em.)

As a meta article, this kind of works (it's still not great), but in-universe it makes no sense whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. It's the Level 5 clearance and the O5 Council parts.

Suggestion:

Notes: This file has been locked for editing to all but personnel in the [name] program. As a reminder: the image included in this file appears unrealistic only as a result of this anomaly and is, in fact, a genuine Foundation archive photo. Further inquiries may be made to Senior Researcher [name].

You could cut it down further, and maybe rephrase, but that's a start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking for a good way to drive home the "yeah, the picture looks fake, dude, but it's supposed to" bit. I presume that overdoes it, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notes: This file has been locked for editing to anyone below Level 5 clearance. As a reminder: the image included in this file appears unrealistic only as a result of this anomaly and is, in fact, a genuine Foundation archive photo. Further attempts to edit this file without O5 approval will not be tolerated. -O5 Council

This lost the upvote right there. Because — bullshit. I don't believe it.

Otherwise, I like this. But no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for replying! If the concept doesn't work for you, then it doesn't work for you! I'm glad it wasn't something I could fix without rewriting the article entirely- I've been asking around to see what's falling flat in people's eyes. Your opinion's legit, and I'm sorry the idea doesn't quite sit well with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the author of this article was polite enough to send me a PM about this article, I will explain my downvote.

This article creates a Foundationverse where a crucial part of the human condition is because of a SCP. People have disbelieved things beyond for longer than 70 years, and making a normal human emotion a SCP does a number on my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yes.

When I saw the picture and the memetic precaution, I was expecting some crazy s*it going on here.

Aand… well, I got a really interesting meta article that actually explains a lot. This is actually pretty great. The Foundation agents need to be given Omicron 12, because they will deal with paranormal stuff all the time and cannot deny that it exists. Coupled with the denial of the recording/autopsy in the logs and the inability to edit the file, I think this used the sci-fi genre for the competition pretty well. Not sure if this should occupy the 2000 slot, but I don't see why it shouldn't. But I liked it and thus I +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but I agree that the final note could be a little more subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its really good in my opinion.

Before I read the article, I kinda laughed at the picture, but as soon as I finished reading, the picture all of a sudden seemed very unsettling to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clever, but the article itself doesn't feel coherent to me. I don't like the way it's structured — it would do well to be more straightforward than this.

Conversely, I also think the final note in the excerpts could be a bit more subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one will prove to be a little divisive, I think, but I like it. Part of that may be my own memetic compulsion to cram grays into my own articles (to mixed results), but I really like how the issues this thing presents in containing it. And there's something delightfully threatening about a life form that responds to being harmed by making you not believe in it anymore.

Also, I enjoy the connection of the Gray here to anomalous phenomena in general, while still maintaining a lot of the mystery that I think makes the concept work. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how the Foundation has to, in essence, fight against what they've been attempting to establish in order to contain this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is clever. Also would make a nice "last measure containment measure" for large information leaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly- this is a very easy thing to test in an organization full of people already studying the anomalous, both to identify the symptomatic and to isolate the immune.

Step 1. Show Class D an obviously anomalous, but safe, object.
Step 2. Expose Class D to recording/photo/slash fanart of Thor from SG-1.
Step 3. Expose Class D to material from the skips they already know are real, as well as like. Batboy articles.

The immune would recognize their own skips and pick out the fakes normally. Everyone else would react with disbelief to something they know is real, as well as the obviously fake stuff.

Also, it'd pick out immune idiots that believe anything you put in front of them. Bonus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yikes. My +1 brought this up from a -1 to a 0. Really, guys?

For some reason, I really liked this one. It felt like a "classic" skip to me in every sense of the word. It hooks into a natural impulse we all feel when we look at the photograph, and it pulls off the "you are already infected" schtick with style and class. It's nothing special, but it's solid and well-written and I enjoyed it.

Side note: I wonder what show the image is from? ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people are just randomly immune.

Also, remember that they had the actual alien cadaver in the next room, and a presumably well-documented paper trail that the photos were legit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Very clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe this is the wrong question to ask…but how would they even identify this phenomenon, much less contain it?
